name: Latinus
description: Latinus, in Roman mythology and tradition, was a king of the Latins, an ancient Italic tribe, for whom Latium was named. He is often depicted as a wise and just ruler, known for his peaceful nature and diplomatic skills. His lineage varies depending on the source, with some claiming him as the son of Faunus and the nymph Marica, while others attribute his parentage to Picus and Canens. Latinus' reign is most famously associated with the arrival of Aeneas and the Trojans in Latium. Initially, Latinus welcomed the Trojans, recognizing them as destined to establish a great empire. He even offered his daughter, Lavinia, in marriage to Aeneas, though she had already been promised to Turnus, king of the Rutulians. This act of hospitality and intended alliance sparked a war between the Trojans and the Rutulians, a conflict central to Virgil's Aeneid. Latinus, despite his desire for peace, found himself drawn into the conflict, ultimately leading to his death. Latinus' legacy is significant as the figure who paved the way for the establishment of the Roman civilization by welcoming Aeneas and setting in motion the events that would lead to the founding of Rome. He represents a pivotal link between the Trojan past and the Roman future, embodying the ideals of diplomacy, hospitality, and the acceptance of destiny. While not a warrior himself, his actions and decisions shaped the course of Roman history and mythology, solidifying his place as a key figure in the Roman foundation narrative. His story highlights the challenges of leadership and the complex interplay of fate, free will, and the consequences of political alliances.
subtitle: King of Latium, Father-in-Law of Aeneas
achievements: Offered Lavinia's hand in marriage to Aeneas
Attempted to maintain peace between Trojans and Rutulians
Established a peaceful and prosperous kingdom
Facilitated the founding of the Roman race

keyMyths.title: Arrival of Aeneas
keyMyths.description: Latinus welcomed Aeneas and the Trojans to Latium, recognizing them as destined to found a great empire. This marked a turning point in the history of the region and set the stage for the founding of Rome. He saw the Trojans as the fulfillment of a prophecy.

keyMyths.title: Betrothal of Lavinia
keyMyths.description: Latinus promised his daughter Lavinia to Aeneas, breaking a previous betrothal to Turnus. This act ignited a war between the Trojans and the Rutulians, highlighting the consequences of political alliances and broken promises. Lavinia was seen as a prize to be won.

keyMyths.title: Latinus' Death
keyMyths.description: While the specific circumstances vary, Latinus dies during the war between the Trojans and Rutulians. His death underscores the tragic consequences of the conflict and the loss of a wise and peaceful ruler. He fought for his kingdom and for what he believed was right.

sources.author: Virgil
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Latinus and the founding of Rome.
sources.text: The primary source for the story of Latinus and the founding of Rome.

sources.author: Dionysius of Halicarnassus
sources.description: Provides an account of early Roman history and mythology, including details of Latinus' reign.
